name: Encausse-les-Thermes and Aspet Loop
description: Cycle 14.6 miles (23.5 km) to the moderate Medieval Ruins of the Hôtel d'Izaux, gaining 1142 feet (348 metres) in 1h 37m.
region_intro: Touring cycling around Encausse-Les-Thermes offers routes through a landscape characterized by rolling hills, river valleys, and views towards the Pyrenees mountains. The region features a mix of open countryside, agricultural land, and occasional forested sections. Elevations vary, providing both gentle gradients along riverbanks and more challenging climbs towards higher passes. Best touring cycling routes around Encausse-Les-Thermes The most popular touring cycling route is Encausse-les-Thermes and Aspet Loop, a 14.6 miles (23.5 km) trail that takes 1 hour 37 minutes to complete. This moderate route offers varied terrain through local villages and countryside. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Route du Cagire – Buret Pass loop from Aspret-Sarrat, a moderate 18.1 miles (29.1 km) path. It features climbs towards mountain passes and views of the surrounding landscape. Local touring cyclists also love the Saint-Paul de Pujos Chapel – The Terreblanque Cave loop from Cabanac-Cazaux, a 17.3 miles (27.8 km) trail leading through varied terrain with historical points of interest, often completed in about 2 hours 25 minutes. Touring cycling around Encausse-Les-Thermes is defined by rolling hills, river valleys, and distant mountain views. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy rides along waterways to more advanced cycling trails with significant elevation gain. The routes in Encausse-Les-Thermes are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 90 reviews. More than 1100 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Encausse-Les-Thermes's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Encausse-Les-Thermes?
A: There are over 160 touring cycling routes in the Encausse-Les-Thermes area, offering a wide range of options for different skill levels and preferences.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in Encausse-Les-Thermes?
A: The region is characterized by rolling hills, picturesque river valleys, and distant views of the Pyrenees mountains. You'll cycle through a mix of open countryside, agricultural land, and occasional forested sections, with varying elevations from gentle riverbank paths to more challenging climbs.

Q: Are there any easy touring cycling routes suitable for beginners or families?
A: Yes, there are 10 easy touring cycling routes in the area. A good option for a more relaxed ride is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/28706994" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Les Thermes d'Encausse – Shady river bank loop from Encausse-les-Thermes&lt;/a&gt;, which is 10.1 miles (16.2 km) long and offers a gentle experience along the river.

Q: What are some challenging touring cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: For experienced touring cyclists seeking a challenge, there are 85 difficult routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39294631" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Saint-Bertrand-de-Comminges – View of the Pyrenees loop from Saint-Gaudens&lt;/a&gt; is a demanding 70.2 miles (112.9 km) route with significant elevation gain, offering expansive views.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in the Encausse-Les-Thermes area?
A: Many routes in the region are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. For example, the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e929419690/encausse-les-thermes-and-aspet-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Encausse-les-Thermes and Aspet Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 14.6 miles (23.5 km) circular route through villages and countryside.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Encausse-Les-Thermes?
A: The spring and autumn months generally offer the most pleasant conditions for touring cycling, with milder temperatures and beautiful scenery. Summer can be warm, especially in the valleys, while winter may bring cooler temperatures and occasional rain, though many routes remain accessible.

Q: What can I see along the touring cycling routes around Encausse-Les-Thermes?
A: The routes often pass by interesting natural and historical landmarks. You might encounter mountain passes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/853251" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Col des Ares&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1530581" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Col de Larrieu (704 m)&lt;/a&gt;, or natural features such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5990187" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lake Saint-Pé-d'Ardet&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/35683218" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Saint-Paul de Pujos Chapel – The Terreblanque Cave loop from Cabanac-Cazaux&lt;/a&gt; also features historical points of interest.

Q: Are there places to stop for refreshments or food along the touring cycling routes?
A: Many routes pass through or near local villages and towns where you can find cafes, bakeries, and small shops to refuel. It's always a good idea to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially on longer or more remote sections.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Encausse-Les-Thermes?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 90 reviews. Reviewers often praise the varied terrain, the charming local villages, and the scenic views of the Pyrenees, noting the good mix of challenging climbs and gentle valley rides.

Q: Is there parking available at the start of the touring cycling routes?
A: Many routes start from towns or villages where public parking is generally available. For specific routes, it's advisable to check the route details on komoot, as starting points often indicate nearby parking options.

Q: Are there any routes that offer views of the Pic de Cagire?
A: Yes, some routes provide excellent vistas of the surrounding peaks. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/26213783" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Route du Cagire – Buret Pass loop from Aspret-Sarrat&lt;/a&gt;, for instance, features climbs towards mountain passes and offers views that include the majestic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/466014" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pic de Cagire&lt;/a&gt;.
tips: A climb by a practicable path in gravel mode. A breathtaking view of the village and the Cagire-Gar massif.

faqs.question: What historical sites or landmarks will I see on this tour?
faqs.answer: The primary highlight of this touring bicycle route is the Medieval Ruins of the Hôtel d'Izaux. Cycling in this region of the Pyrenees also offers glimpses of charming villages and the stunning natural backdrop of the mountains.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the route to Izaux?
faqs.answer: This route is designed for touring bicycles, suggesting a mix of well-surfaced roads and potentially some quieter lanes or off-road tracks. While the Pyrenees can be challenging, many routes in the Pre-Pyrenees, like this one, are described as not overly demanding, offering a pleasant cycling experience.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best times to cycle in the Pre-Pyrenees, including this route, are generally spring and autumn. During these seasons, you can expect pleasant temperatures and comfortable conditions for touring cycling.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the trail?
faqs.answer: While specific parking for this exact route isn't detailed, areas like La Barthe-de-Neste or Lannemezan, which are common starting points for cycling in the Hautes-Pyrénées, typically offer parking options suitable for cyclists. It's advisable to check local parking availability upon arrival.

faqs.question: Is this trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Information specifically regarding dogs on this particular cycling route is not available. Generally, cycling routes in the Pyrenees may allow dogs, but it's always best to check local regulations or signage regarding leash rules and access for pets, especially near historical sites or protected natural areas.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: There are no specific permits or entrance fees required for cycling this route. Access to the general cycling paths and the medieval ruins themselves is typically free, allowing you to enjoy the historical and natural beauty without additional charges.

faqs.question: Is this a family-friendly cycling route?
faqs.answer: Given its 'moderate' difficulty and suitability for touring bicycles, this route could be considered family-friendly for those with some cycling experience. The region offers many routes along quiet lanes, which are ideal for a more relaxed family outing. However, always assess your family's fitness and comfort with the stated elevation gain.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route intersects with several other paths. You'll find sections that overlap with official trails such as Du Job au Ger, Pedals d'Occitània, Chemin de la Carretère, and various local rural paths like Chemin rural dit du Barote and Chemin d'Escaig.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or stay near the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, towns along routes in this region, such as La Barthe-de-Neste and Lannemezan, offer various options for food and accommodation. You'll find cafes, restaurants, and places to stay, meaning you don't need to carry excessive supplies for your tour.

faqs.question: What makes this cycling route unique?
faqs.answer: This route offers a unique blend of history and nature. You get to explore the rich heritage of the Pyrenees, cycling towards ancient sites like the Medieval Ruins of the Hôtel d'Izaux, all while enjoying the tranquil beauty of the landscape, often on quiet lanes with minimal traffic.

faqs.question: Is this a loop route?
faqs.answer: The provided tour data does not explicitly state if this is a loop. However, many touring bicycle routes in the Pyrenees are designed as point-to-point or can be combined with other paths to form a loop.
